examples.title: Algorithm: Producer-Consumer with Bounded Buffer
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the producer-consumer problem using a circular bounded buffer with two pointers (in and out)
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the classic producer-consumer problem using a bounded circular buffer. The producer generates items and places them in the buffer, while the consumer removes and processes them. The circular buffer uses two pointers: 'in' for the next free position where producer writes, and 'out' for the first full position from which consumer reads. The condition ((in + 1) % N) == out indicates the buffer is full, while in == out indicates it is empty. One slot is kept empty to distinguish between full and empty states.

examples.title: Python: Producer-Consumer with Message Queue
examples.description: Python implementation using multiprocessing Queue for inter-process communication via message passing
examples.code:
"""
Producer-Consumer Problem using Python Multiprocessing
Demonstrates Message Passing IPC mechanism
"""

import multiprocessing
import time
import random

# Constants
BUFFER_SIZE = 5
NUM_ITEMS = 10

def producer(queue, num_items):
 """
 Producer process: generates items and places them in the queue.
 """
 for i in range(num_items):
 # Simulate item production
 item = f"Item-{i}-{random.randint(1000, 9999)}"
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3)) # Simulate production time
 
 # Put item in queue (blocks if queue is full)
 queue.put(item)
 print(f"[PRODUCER] Produced: {item} | Queue size: {queue.qsize()}")
 
 # Sentinel value to signal consumer to stop
 queue.put(None)
 print("[PRODUCER] Finished producing - sent STOP signal")

def consumer(queue, num_items):
 """
 Consumer process: retrieves and consumes items from the queue.
 """
 consumed_count = 0
 
 while consumed_count < num_items:
 # Get item from queue (blocks if queue is empty)
 item = queue.get()
 
 if item is None: # Stop signal received
 print("[CONSUMER] Received STOP signal")
 break
 
 time.sleep(random.uniform(0.2, 0.4)) # Simulate processing time
 
 print(f"[CONSUMER] Consumed: {item} | Queue size: {queue.qsize()}")
 consumed_count += 1
 
 print(f"[CONSUMER] Finished - consumed {consumed_count} items")

def main():
 """
 Main function to demonstrate IPC using message passing.
 """
 print("=" * 60)
 print("IPC Demonstration: Producer-Consumer with Message Queue")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 # Create a bounded queue (Message Passing IPC)
 # maxsize=BUFFER_SIZE makes it a bounded buffer
 message_queue = multiprocessing.Queue(maxsize=BUFFER_SIZE)
 
 # Create producer and consumer processes
 producer_process = multiprocessing.Process(
 target=producer, 
 args=(message_queue, NUM_ITEMS)
 )
 consumer_process = multiprocessing.Process(
 target=consumer, 
 args=(message_queue, NUM_ITEMS)
 )
 
 # Start both processes
 print("\nStarting Producer and Consumer processes...\n")
 producer_process.start()
 consumer_process.start()
 
 # Wait for both processes to complete
 producer_process.join()
 consumer_process.join()
 
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("IPC Complete! Both processes finished successfully.")
 print("=" * 60)

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()

# =====================================================================
# Explanation:
# 
# 1. multiprocessing.Queue implements the MESSAGE PASSING IPC model
# 2. The Queue handles all synchronization internally (blocking put/get)
# 3. Bounded queue (maxsize) acts as a bounded buffer - producer blocks
# when full, consumer blocks when empty
# 4. This demonstrates how processes communicate WITHOUT sharing memory
# 5. The OS kernel manages the message passing through the queue
# 
# Key IPC Concepts Demonstrated:
# - Message Passing Model (vs Shared Memory)
# - Blocking send/receive operations
# - Bounded Buffer with flow control
# - Process synchronization via message queue
# =====================================================================
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the Message Passing IPC model using multiprocessing.Queue. The queue acts as a communication channel between the producer and consumer processes. Key features: (1) The Queue handles synchronization internally - put() blocks when full, get() blocks when empty, (2) maxsize creates a bounded buffer preventing overflow, (3) A sentinel value (None) signals the consumer to terminate. This demonstrates how processes can communicate without directly sharing memory - the kernel manages the message queue.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for put and get operations (amortized)

examples.title: C: Producer-Consumer with Shared Memory and Semaphores
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating shared memory IPC with semaphores for synchronization in the producer-consumer problem
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the Shared Memory IPC model with System V semaphores for synchronization. The shared buffer is created using shmget() and attached to both processes' address spaces using shmat(). Three semaphores control synchronization: sem_empty tracks empty slots, sem_full tracks filled slots, and sem_mutex ensures mutual exclusion. The producer waits for an empty slot, writes to the shared buffer, then signals a full slot. The consumer does the reverse. This is a classic demonstration of how cooperating processes share data through shared memory while maintaining synchronization.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(BUFFER_SIZE) for shared memory buffer + O(1) for semaphore structures

examples.title: C: Message Passing using POSIX Message Queues
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating message passing IPC using POSIX message queues for inter-process communication
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the Message Passing IPC model using POSIX message queues. Unlike shared memory where processes directly access common memory, message passing uses the kernel as an intermediary. The mq_open() creates a queue, mq_send() puts messages in (blocking if full), and mq_receive() retrieves them (blocking if empty). Messages include a type field for selective receiving and a priority. This is the classic message queue approach used in modern systems for asynchronous inter-process communication.
